cards.title: Stop Playing Code Archaeologist: AI for 'Why,' Not Just 'What' in Dev Teams
cards.thesis: AI is incredible at generating code and helping build new things, but developers are still wasting weeks trying to understand the 'why' behind existing architectural decisions and code choices. This gap in institutional knowledge (the story and reasoning behind how a system was built) is a huge pain point for new team members and existing engineers alike, and current AI tools aren't solving it.

evidence.text: We onboarded a senior engineer recently... He spent 3 weeks playing code archaeologist just to understand WHY our codebase looks the way it does. Not what the code does. That was fast. But the reasoning behind decisions: Why Redis over in-memory cache? Why GraphQL for this one service but REST everywhere else?

What's your biggest pain point when joining a new developer team? ...new coders in an org, have plenty of questions about the org's framework or operations in general. This makes them ask their seniors which they might not really like due to the wastage of time...
AI coding tools generate code very quickly, but they almost never generate full end to end test coverage. they create a ton of tests mostly unit and intergations but real user scenarios are missing.

I saw a post about someone converting an entire C++ codebase to Rust using AI in under two weeks. That inspired me — if AI can rewrite a whole language stack that fast, I wanted to try building a programming language from scratch with AI assistance.

cards.opportunity: New engineers are still spending weeks digging through old tickets and Slack threads to understand *why* a piece of code looks the way it does, even with AI generating the 'what.' Someone needs to build an AI assistant that ingests all that messy historical context—PRs, tickets, discussions—and instantly explains the *reasoning* behind any code block. You could start with a simple VS Code extension that connects to GitHub and Jira APIs, giving developers instant answers to 'why this, not that?'

cards.title: The Goldilocks Zone of Image Editing: Why Simple, Powerful Tools Are Blowing Up
cards.thesis: There's a clear demand for image editing tools that are more capable than basic built-in apps (like MS Paint) but far simpler and less overwhelming than professional software (like Photoshop or Paint.net). GrobPaint is getting attention for hitting this sweet spot, proving builders want 'just right' tools for quick, impactful visuals.

evidence.text: GrobPaint is a new tool positioned 'Somewhere Between MS Paint and Paint.net,' indicating a market for moderately featured, easy-to-use image editors.
The project description on GitHub reiterates its niche: 'Somewhere between MS Paint and Paint.NET. Multiplatform by default,' highlighting its accessibility across different operating systems.

The consistent messaging and engagement on both Hacker News (47 points) and GitHub suggest a significant interest in tools that bridge the gap between overly simplistic and overly complex image manipulation.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's excited about GrobPaint finding the perfect middle ground for general image editing, but the real untapped market is a 'GrobPaint-for-builders.' Imagine a dead-simple tool that takes your app screenshots, lets you quickly annotate, blur sensitive info, and then auto-formats them for social media, landing pages, or documentation. You could ship this as a browser extension or simple desktop app in a weekend, directly addressing the pain point of creating quick, professional-looking marketing assets without needing a design degree.
